text:
BREAST MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
ROUTINE SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Maria Elena Reyes
AGE: 46 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IIB (cT2N1M0 -> ypT1cN1M0) triple-negative breast cancer, left breast, grade 3, invasive ductal carcinoma; non-pCR post-neoadjuvant therapy (RCB-II); status post left partial mastectomy with ALND; status post adjuvant radiation, capecitabine, and pembrolizumab (all completed)
VISIT TYPE: Routine oncology surveillance, approximately 18 months post-surgery

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Reyes presents for her scheduled 18-month post-operative surveillance visit. She reports she is feeling well overall and has no new symptoms or concerns today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes is a 46-year-old premenopausal Hispanic woman with a history of Stage IIB triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) of the left breast, diagnosed at age 45. Her tumor was grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR negative, HER2 1+ by IHC (non-amplified by FISH), Ki-67 85%, with a notably high PD-L1 Combined Positive Score of 18 (22C3 antibody) and a complex genomic profile on FoundationOne CDx including TP53 p.R248W, PTEN loss, MYC amplification, and RB1 loss. She presented with a 3.2 cm palpable left upper outer quadrant mass and clinically suspicious left axillary adenopathy; workup confirmed cT2N1M0 disease.

She was enrolled in the neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy approach per KEYNOTE-522, receiving pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks in combination with dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC x 4 cycles, q2 weeks) followed by weekly paclitaxel x 12 doses over approximately 20 weeks total. Her course was complicated by Grade 2 nausea and alopecia during the AC phase, Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy with dose reduction of paclitaxel (to 64 mg/m2 for weeks 9-12), and a pembrolizumab-related Grade 2 immune arthralgia involving the bilateral knees and wrists requiring a one-cycle hold and a prednisone taper, after which pembrolizumab was successfully resumed.

Post-neoadjuvant breast MRI showed a partial radiographic response with the left breast mass reducing from 3.5 cm to 1.4 cm and near-normalization of left axillary lymph nodes. She subsequently underwent left partial mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and completion axillary lymph node dissection. Pathology confirmed ypT1cN1M0 disease (1.2 cm residual invasive ductal carcinoma; 3/16 lymph nodes positive, largest deposit 0.8 cm, no extranodal extension; Miller-Payne grade 3, RCB score 2.8, RCB-II). No pathologic complete response was achieved. Persistent TNBC phenotype on residual tumor IHC.

Given non-pCR per CREATE-X trial rationale, she was started on adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 every 21 days (dose reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID from Cycle 3 onward for Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome/palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) and continued pembrolizumab to complete 1 year of total pembro therapy (~17 doses total across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases). She also received adjuvant radiation to the left breast and regional lymphatics (whole breast 50 Gy + 10 Gy tumor bed boost; regional nodal irradiation to supraclavicular, axillary levels I-III, and internal mammary chain), complicated by Grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation and a mild Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis managed conservatively with a short prednisone course.

During the adjuvant phase, she developed pembrolizumab-related immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH peak 7.8 mIU/L), managed with levothyroxine, ultimately dose-stabilized at 75 mcg daily with TSH normalization. Pembrolizumab was continued without interruption for the hypothyroidism per protocol guidance. She completed all 8 cycles of capecitabine and all 17 planned pembrolizumab doses approximately 12 months ago.

Surveillance to date: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 6 months post-surgery showed no evidence of disease (NED), with resolving left lung post-radiation changes. CT CAP and bilateral breast MRI at 12 months post-surgery were both without evidence of local recurrence or distant metastasis. Breast MRI performed at 18 months (obtained and reviewed in preparation for today's visit) again showed no suspicious new enhancement, stable post-operative and post-radiation changes in the left breast, and no pathologic axillary adenopathy bilaterally.

Today at 18 months post-surgery, Ms. Reyes reports feeling well. She has returned to full-time teaching. She no longer has peripheral neuropathy symptoms, which resolved completely over several months after completion of paclitaxel. Hand-foot syndrome has fully resolved. Her hair has fully regrown. She is well-compensated on levothyroxine. She mentions mild fatigue intermittently but describes it as manageable and improved compared to prior visits. She has been actively engaged in a support group for young women with breast cancer and continues individual psychotherapy. She reports normal menstrual cycles have resumed (chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea resolved at approximately 9 months post-completion of chemotherapy). She was referred to reproductive endocrinology previously for counseling regarding ovarian function and pregnancy safety post-TNBC; she underwent that evaluation and is in ongoing discussion with her RE.

She had an expanded germline panel sent at the 12-month visit. Results confirmed BRCA1 and BRCA2 wildtype, PALB2 wildtype, RAD51C and RAD51D wildtype, BRIP1 wildtype, ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance, classification unchanged since original testing). Genetic counselor reviewed these results with the patient at a recent appointment; no change in surgical or screening management was recommended on the basis of the ATM VUS.

She also had a baseline echocardiogram at the 12-month visit (LVEF 62%, normal), obtained for cardiovascular surveillance given her prior anthracycline exposure.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild intermittent fatigue, improved from prior. No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Appetite intact.
Integumentary: No skin rashes, new lesions, or palmar-plantar changes. Hair has fully regrown (Grade 0 alopecia).
Neurological: No numbness, tingling, or burning in the hands or feet. Bilateral neuropathy fully resolved. No new focal neurological complaints.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or swelling. Resolved from prior pembrolizumab-related arthralgia. No new bone pain or back pain.
Pulmonary: Occasional mild dry cough, significantly improved from prior. No dyspnea at rest or with exertion. No pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: No diarrhea, constipation, nausea, or vomiting. No abdominal pain. No blood in stool.
Endocrine: No palpitations, heat or cold intolerance, or changes in bowel habits suggestive of thyroid dysfunction. Taking levothyroxine daily without issue.
Genitourinary/Gynecologic: Menstrual cycles regular and resumed. No pelvic pain. No unusual vaginal bleeding.
Lymphatic: No palpable new lumps in the breasts, axillae, or elsewhere.
Psychiatric: Reports some ongoing cancer-related anxiety, managed actively in therapy. No active depression or suicidal ideation. Quality of life described as good and improving.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 degrees C, weight 68.2 kg, BMI 26.1. O2 saturation 98% on room air.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Hispanic woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Fully regrown hair noted. Appears well.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.

Neck/Thyroid: Neck supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated bilaterally. Thyroid not enlarged, non-tender, no palpable nodules.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Breast Examination:
- Left breast: Well-healed curvilinear lumpectomy scar in the upper outer quadrant. Mild post-radiation skin changes present (slightly indurated, faintly hyperpigmented overlying the surgical scar), no erythema, warmth, or skin breakdown. No palpable mass, nodule, or thickening in the left breast. No nipple discharge.
- Right breast: No palpable mass, nodule, skin dimpling, or nipple discharge.
- Bilateral axillae: No palpable axillary adenopathy. Left axillary surgical changes appreciated without evidence of new or recurrent nodal disease. No right axillary adenopathy.

Extremities: No peripheral edema bilaterally. Left arm: no clinical lymphedema on visual inspection or circumferential tape measure comparison to right; patient reports no arm heaviness or swelling. Sensation intact in bilateral hands and feet; no residual paresthesias on exam.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal.

Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling, warmth, or tenderness in bilateral knees or wrists. Full range of motion.

Skin: No new rashes, lesions, or vitiligo patches noted. Mild post-radiation hyperpigmentation and induration of left anterior chest as noted above.

Performance Status: ECOG 0. Karnofsky 90-100%.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC with differential:
- WBC 6.1 x 10^9/L (normal), with normal differential
- Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL (normal)
- Platelets 221 x 10^9/L (normal)
- ANC 3.8 x 10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL
- AST 22 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL (all within normal limits)
- Glucose 89 mg/dL
- Albumin 4.3 g/dL

Thyroid function:
- TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal reference range 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) -- stable and within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
- Free T4 1.2 ng/dL (normal)

Lipid panel (obtained per cardiovascular surveillance guidelines, given anthracycline exposure):
- Total cholesterol 178 mg/dL, LDL 102 mg/dL, HDL 58 mg/dL, Triglycerides 91 mg/dL (all within normal limits)

Tumor markers: Not routinely obtained per current NCCN guidelines for surveillance in early-stage breast cancer (not clinically indicated in the absence of symptoms or imaging findings suggesting recurrence).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Bilateral breast MRI (obtained prior to today's visit, reviewed in full):
Left breast demonstrates the expected post-lumpectomy and post-radiation changes at the surgical site in the upper outer quadrant -- there is architectural distortion and mild skin thickening overlying the scar, both stable and consistent with expected healing. No suspicious new enhancement is identified in the left breast. The right breast shows no suspicious enhancement, no mass, and no architectural distortion. Bilateral axillary lymph nodes are normal in morphology without pathologic enlargement. Compared to the 12-month post-surgical MRI, no interval change. Interpreted as no evidence of local recurrence.

Prior surveillance imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 12 months post-surgery): Previously reviewed, without evidence of distant metastatic disease; not repeated today given low pretest probability and stable interval clinical picture.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER, STAGE IIB (ypT1cN1M0 after neoadjuvant therapy), NON-PCR, STATUS POST COMPLETION OF ALL PLANNED THERAPY -- NOW 18 MONTHS POST-SURGERY, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE

Ms. Reyes is a 46-year-old woman with a prior Stage IIB TNBC, grade 3, who had a partial but not complete pathologic response to neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/ddAC/paclitaxel (RCB-II), then underwent left partial mastectomy and ALND (ypT1cN1M0, 3/16 positive nodes), followed by adjuvant radiation, capecitabine (completed 8 cycles, dose-reduced for HFS), and completion of 1 year of pembrolizumab (17 total doses). She is now 18 months from surgery, with all adjuvant therapy completed approximately 6 months ago.

Today's visit, bilateral breast MRI, and recent labs are all without evidence of local or distant recurrence. She is clinically well with ECOG PS 0. This is an encouraging milestone. We discussed that the highest risk period for TNBC recurrence is within the first 3 years from completion of therapy, and that ongoing close surveillance is important during this window.

- Continue surveillance per schedule: clinic visits every 3-4 months through the 2-year mark (approximately 6 months from now), then transition to every 6 months through year 5.
- Annual bilateral breast MRI and mammography per NCCN high-risk guidelines (next annual MRI and mammogram due at approximately 24 months post-surgery; today's 18-month MRI reviewed and clear).
- No additional CT imaging indicated at this time in the absence of symptoms; next scheduled CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be obtained at 24 months post-surgery or sooner if any clinical concern arises.
- Patient instructed on symptoms warranting earlier evaluation: new breast changes or skin changes in the left chest/scar area, new lymphadenopathy, persistent bone pain, new neurological symptoms, unexplained weight loss, or dyspnea.

2. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (PEMBROLIZUMAB-RELATED)

TSH is 2.1 mIU/L today -- stable and within normal limits on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Patient is asymptomatic. This is an expected and manageable long-term sequela of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
- Repeat TSH at next visit (approximately 3-4 months).
- No dose change indicated today.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (PACLITAXEL-RELATED) -- RESOLVED

Grade 0 peripheral neuropathy on exam and by history. Complete resolution is gratifying and consistent with the expected course in younger patients following dose-reduced weekly paclitaxel. No treatment needed; continue monitoring.

4. CARDIOVASCULAR SURVEILLANCE -- ANTHRACYCLINE EXPOSURE

Baseline echocardiogram at 12 months post-surgery showed LVEF 62% (normal). Lipid panel today within normal limits. No symptoms of cardiac dysfunction. Patient should have repeat echocardiogram at 5 years post-anthracycline exposure per cardio-oncology guidelines; reminder placed in the chart.

5. LYMPHEDEMA SURVEILLANCE

No clinical lymphedema on exam today. Arm circumferences symmetric. Patient has had ALND with removal of 16 axillary nodes, which places her at long-term risk for left arm lymphedema. She has been counseled previously on lymphedema precautions (avoiding blood draws, blood pressure cuffs, and IV access in the left arm; maintaining healthy weight; seeking prompt evaluation of any arm swelling).

- Continue lymphedema monitoring at each visit.
- Referral to lymphedema therapist or physical therapy if any early signs develop.

6. GERMLINE GENETICS AND FAMILY HISTORY

Expanded germline panel reviewed: BRCA1/2 wildtype, PALB2 wildtype, RAD51C/D wildtype, BRIP1 wildtype; ATM p.V2424G remains a VUS. Genetic counseling has been completed. No change in clinical management recommended at this time. A maternal cousin's recent diagnosis of ovarian cancer was the impetus for expanded testing; panel is appropriately comprehensive. ATM VUS to be tracked via ClinVar and professional databases for potential reclassification; genetic counselor will follow up with patient if reclassification occurs.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND QUALITY OF LIFE

Patient is engaged in individual psychotherapy for cancer-related anxiety and body image concerns. She participates in a peer support group for young women with breast cancer. She describes quality of life as good and improving. No active depression or suicidal ideation. She has returned to full-time work as a high school teacher, which she finds meaningful and normalizing.

- Continue to support access to psychosocial resources.
- NCCN distress thermometer administered today; score 3/10 (mild), primarily related to worry about recurrence -- appropriate discussion provided regarding the favorable direction of her surveillance data and importance of ongoing monitoring.

8. REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH AND FERTILITY

Menstrual cycles have resumed following chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea (resolved at approximately 9 months). This is expected and favorable in a premenopausal woman of her age. Patient was referred to reproductive endocrinology at the 12-month visit; ongoing discussions are occurring regarding ovarian function preservation and safety of future pregnancy post-TNBC. Standard guidance counsels that pregnancy after TNBC is not contraindicated and does not appear to worsen oncologic outcomes, though timing considerations and the 2-3 year observation window post-treatment are relevant given her intermediate-high risk profile (RCB-II, node-positive). RE and oncology are in communication.

9. LEVOTHYROXINE REFILL

Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily -- refill provided for 90-day supply with one refill.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in 3 months for routine surveillance visit. Patient has contact information for the triage nurse line and instructed to call sooner for any new or concerning symptoms.

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and coordination: approximately 45 minutes.

--- 
Electronically signed by the Attending Physician, Breast Medical Oncology
